questions.q: What is the meaning of Not-for-Profit Organisations? Explain their characteristics and objectives. Also, state the differences between Not-for-Profit Organisations and business enterprises.
questions.answer: Not-for-Profit Organisations are entities that are formed for the purpose of providing services to their members or to the community at large, without any intention of making a profit. The main characteristics of Not-for-Profit Organisations are that they are non-profit making, they have separate legal entity, and they are managed by a group of persons known as trustees or board of directors. The main objectives of Not-for-Profit Organisations are to provide services to their members or to the community, to promote social welfare, and to undertake charitable activities. The main differences between Not-for-Profit Organisations and business enterprises are that Not-for-Profit Organisations are non-profit making, while business enterprises are profit making, Not-for-Profit Organisations have separate legal entity, while business enterprises may or may not have separate legal entity, and Not-for-Profit Organisations are managed by a group of persons known as trustees or board of directors, while business enterprises are managed by owners or shareholders.
marking_scheme: Definition and explanation of Not-for-Profit Organisations (1 mark)
Differences between Not-for-Profit Organisations and business enterprises (2 marks)

questions.q: From the following information, prepare the Income and Expenditure Account for the year ended 31st March 2022: Subscription received during the year Rs. 50,000, Donation received for a specific purpose Rs. 20,000, Rent paid Rs. 10,000, Salary paid Rs. 30,000, Miscellaneous expenses Rs. 5,000, and Surplus on revaluation of investments Rs. 15,000.
questions.answer:
Calculation:
Income and Expenditure Account for the year ended 31st March 2022
Income
  Subscription (50,000)
  Donation for specific purpose (20,000)
  Surplus on revaluation of investments (15,000)
Total Income (85,000)
Expenditure
  Rent (10,000)
  Salary (30,000)
  Miscellaneous expenses (5,000)
Total Expenditure (45,000)
Surplus (85,000 - 45,000) (40,000)
marking_scheme: Preparation of Income and Expenditure Account (4 marks)
questions.topic: Preparation of Income and Expenditure Account

questions.q: A charitable trust has the following receipts and payments during the year ended 31st March 2022: Receipts - Donation Rs. 1,00,000, Subscription Rs. 20,000, Interest on investments Rs. 15,000. Payments - Salary Rs. 30,000, Rent Rs. 10,000, Miscellaneous expenses Rs. 5,000. Prepare the Receipts and Payments Account for the year ended 31st March 2022.

Calculation:
Receipts and Payments Account for the year ended 31st March 2022
Receipts
  Donation (1,00,000)
  Subscription (20,000)
  Interest on investments (15,000)
Total Receipts (1,35,000)
Payments
  Salary (30,000)
  Rent (10,000)

Total Payments (45,000)
Surplus (1,35,000 - 45,000) (90,000)

questions.q: What is the difference between capital and revenue expenditure? Explain with examples. Also, state the accounting treatment of capital and revenue expenditure in the books of Not-for-Profit Organisations.
questions.answer: Capital expenditure is the expenditure that is incurred to acquire or improve the assets of the organisation, while revenue expenditure is the expenditure that is incurred to meet the day-to-day expenses of the organisation. Examples of capital expenditure are purchase of land, purchase of equipment, and construction of building. Examples of revenue expenditure are salary, rent, and miscellaneous expenses. In the books of Not-for-Profit Organisations, capital expenditure is recorded as an asset, while revenue expenditure is recorded as an expense in the Income and Expenditure Account.
marking_scheme: Definition and explanation of capital and revenue expenditure (1 mark)
Examples of capital and revenue expenditure (1 mark)
Accounting treatment of capital and revenue expenditure (3 marks)
questions.topic: Accounting Treatment of Capital and Revenue Expenditure

questions.q: A Not-for-Profit Organisation has the following information for the year ended 31st March 2022: Opening stock of stationery Rs. 5,000, Purchases during the year Rs. 20,000, and Closing stock of stationery Rs. 10,000. Prepare the Accounting Entry to record the consumption of stationery during the year.
questions.answer:
Calculation:
Accounting Entry to record the consumption of stationery during the year:
Debit: Stationery Consumption Account (20,000 + 5,000 - 10,000) Rs. 15,000
Credit: Cash/Bank Account (20,000)
Credit: Opening Stock of Stationery Account (5,000)
Debit: Closing Stock of Stationery Account (10,000)
marking_scheme: Calculation of consumption of stationery (2 marks)
Accounting entry to record the consumption of stationery (3 marks)
questions.topic: Accounting for Consumption of Materials and Stationery
